origin.question:
Consider a stationary spacetime possessing a timelike Killing vector field $\xi^{\alpha}$. The Komar mass associated with this spacetime is defined on a closed 2-dimensional spacelike surface $S$ as the surface integral:$$M_K = - rac{1}{8\pi} \oint_S 
abla^{\alpha} \xi^{ eta} dS_{\alpha eta}$$where $dS_{\alpha eta}$ is the surface element of $S$. Using the generalized Stokes theorem and the Einstein field equations $R_{\alpha eta} -  rac{1}{2} g_{\alpha eta} R = 8\pi T_{\alpha eta}$ (where $T_{\alpha eta}$ is the energy-momentum tensor and $R$ is its trace $T$), derive the expression for the Komar mass as a 3-dimensional volume integral over a spacelike hypersurface $V$ bounded by $S$, expressed in terms of $T_{\alpha eta}$, $R$, and $\xi^{\alpha}$. (Hint: You will need the Killing vector identity $
abla_{\alpha} 
abla_{ eta} \xi_{\gamma} = R_{\gamma eta\alpha\delta} \xi^{\delta}$)
origin.solution:
1. Apply the generalized Stokes theorem to convert the 2-surface integral into a 3-volume integral over the spacelike hypersurface $V$ whose boundary is $S$:$$\oint_S 
abla^{\alpha} \xi^{ eta} dS_{\alpha eta} = \int_V 
abla_{ eta} (
abla^{\alpha} \xi^{ eta}) d\Sigma_{\alpha}$$2. Evaluate the covariant derivative divergence term $
abla_{ eta} (
abla^{\alpha} \xi^{ eta})$. Using the metric to lower the index, this is $g^{\alpha\gamma} 
abla_{ eta} 
abla_{\gamma} \xi^{ eta}$. By the Killing vector identity provided, we have $
abla_{ eta} 
abla_{\gamma} \xi^{ eta} = R^{ eta}{}_{\gamma eta\delta} \xi^{\delta} = R_{\gamma\delta} \xi^{\delta}$. Therefore:$$
abla_{ eta} (
abla^{\alpha} \xi^{ eta}) = g^{\alpha\gamma} R_{\gamma\delta} \xi^{\delta} = R^{\alpha}{}_{\delta} \xi^{\delta}$$3. Substitute the Einstein field equations $R_{\alpha eta} -  rac{1}{2} g_{\alpha eta} R = 8\pi T_{\alpha eta}$ to express the Ricci tensor in terms of the energy-momentum tensor:$$R^{\alpha}{}_{\delta} = 8\pi \left( T^{\alpha}{}_{\delta} -  rac{1}{2} g^{\alpha}{}_{\delta} Right)$$Contracting with the Killing vector $\xi^{\delta}$ gives:$$R^{\alpha}{}_{\delta} \xi^{\delta} = 8\pi \left( T^{\alpha}{}_{\delta} \xi^{\delta} -  rac{1}{2} \xi^{\alpha} Right)$$4. Substitute this result back into the volume integral:$$\oint_S 
abla^{\alpha} \xi^{ eta} dS_{\alpha eta} = 8\pi \int_V \left( T^{\alpha}{}_{\delta} \xi^{\delta} -  rac{1}{2} \xi^{\alpha} Right) d\Sigma_{\alpha}$$5. Finally, plug this into the definition of Komar mass:$$M_K = - rac{1}{8\pi} \left[ 8\pi \int_V \left( T^{\alpha}{}_{ eta} \xi^{ eta} -  rac{1}{2} \xi^{\alpha} Right) d\Sigma_{\alpha}ight] = -\int_V \left( T^{\alpha}{}_{ eta} \xi^{ eta} -  rac{1}{2} \xi^{\alpha} Right) d\Sigma_{\alpha}$$This demonstrates the Komar mass volume integral expression, revealing the trace correction term that distinguishes it from the simple energy current integral.
